title: Angular Advanced — Paper 5
description: Advanced Angular concepts: reactive forms, custom validators, routing, guards, HTTP client, RxJS, change detection, custom directives/pipes, state management, and testing.

options.text: The form will throw an error if any control is set to null
When reset() is called, controls restore to their initial values rather than null
The form ignores null values sent from the server
Controls cannot be disabled programmatically

questions.text: What is the purpose of the FormGroup's addControl() method?

options.text: Adds a validator to an existing control
Dynamically adds a new named FormControl (or nested FormGroup/FormArray) to the FormGroup at runtime
Adds a control to a FormArray
Registers a control with a template-driven form directive
To save the validators to local storage
To trigger re-validation with the new validators immediately, since setValidators() alone does not re-run validation
To mark the control as touched
To emit the new value to the parent FormGroup
new FormControl('', forbiddenValue)
new FormControl('', forbiddenValue('admin'))
new FormControl('', [forbiddenValue, 'admin'])
new FormControl('', { validator: forbiddenValue('admin') })

questions.text: Which of the following correctly implements an AsyncValidatorFn that checks if a username is taken?

options.text: return (c) => this.userService.checkUsername(c.value);
return (c: AbstractControl): Observable<ValidationErrors | null> => this.userService.checkUsername(c.value).pipe(map(taken => taken ? { usernameTaken: true } : null));
return (c) => { this.userService.checkUsername(c.value).subscribe(taken => taken ? { usernameTaken: true } : null); };
return async (c) => await this.userService.checkUsername(c.value);
this.router.data['title']
this.route.snapshot.data['title']
this.route.params['title']
this.route.queryParams['title']

questions.text: What is the RouterLinkActive directive used for?

options.text: Programmatically navigating to a route
Adding a CSS class to an element when its associated routerLink matches the current active URL
Lazy loading a component when a link is clicked
Detecting if a route is guarded

questions.text: In Angular, what is the functional router guard syntax introduced in Angular 15+ and what replaces CanActivate class-based guards?

options.text: Functional guards replace class-based guards; you define a plain function of type CanActivateFn that can inject services using inject()
Both functional and class-based guards work identically with no syntax difference
Class-based guards were replaced by route interceptors
Functional guards can only redirect; class-based guards can block navigation

questions.text: What does withPreloading(PreloadAllModules) do when passed to provideRouter()?

options.text: Disables lazy loading for all modules
Preloads all lazy-loaded modules in the background after the initial application load completes
Loads only the module for the current route
Compiles all lazy modules at build time
Blocking all non-GET requests
Caching GET responses: returning from cache if available, otherwise making the real request and caching the result
Adding cache-control headers to all requests
Clearing the cache after each response

questions.text: What is the purpose of HttpParams in Angular HttpClient?

options.text: To configure request timeout settings
To build immutable URL query string parameters in a type-safe, chainable way for HTTP requests
To parse URL path parameters from the response
To set HTTP headers for the request
It replays the search 1 time if it errors
It multicasts the observable, shares it among multiple subscribers, and replays the last 1 emission to new late subscribers
It caches only the first search result
It buffers 1 emission before forwarding to subscribers

questions.text: What is the difference between Subject and AsyncSubject in RxJS?

options.text: Subject emits asynchronously; AsyncSubject emits synchronously
AsyncSubject only emits the last value and only when the sequence completes; Subject emits every value as it arrives
AsyncSubject supports multiple simultaneous subscribers; Subject does not
They are identical but AsyncSubject has an automatic timeout

questions.text: In Angular, what is the zoneless change detection mode and how is it enabled?

options.text: It removes all change detection; enabled with ChangeDetectionStrategy.None
It eliminates the dependency on Zone.js and relies on signals and explicit detectChanges() calls; enabled by passing provideExperimentalZonelessChangeDetection() to bootstrapApplication
It runs change detection outside the browser; enabled with NgZone.runOutsideAngular()
It is a Webpack configuration that removes Zone.js from the bundle

questions.text: Given an OnPush component that displays data from a service via a manually managed subscription (not async pipe), what must you do after updating the data property from the subscription callback?

options.text: Call NgZone.runOutsideAngular()
Call this.cdr.markForCheck() to schedule the component for checking in the next change detection cycle
Use setTimeout() to defer the update
Nothing — OnPush automatically detects service changes
Focuses the element on every change detection; AfterViewInit fires before the template is rendered
Focuses the host element after the view is fully initialized; AfterViewInit fires after the component's template and children are rendered, ensuring the element exists in the DOM
Focuses the element on every click; AfterViewInit handles click events
Focuses the first input inside the element; AfterViewInit runs after HTTP calls complete
Pure — the input date reference does not change so pure is fine
Impure — the transform output changes over time even when the input date reference stays the same, so it must re-run on each change detection cycle
Pure — Angular caches dates automatically
Neither — time-based pipes must use setTimeout internally instead

questions.text: In NgRx, what is the difference between a Feature Store and the Root Store?

options.text: Feature stores cannot have effects; root stores can
The root store is registered with StoreModule.forRoot/provideStore; feature stores are registered with StoreModule.forFeature/provideState and are typically lazy-loaded with their feature module
Feature stores are synchronous; root store is asynchronous
Root store uses reducers; feature stores use only selectors

questions.text: In Angular Signals, what happens when you call effect() and one of the signals it reads changes?

options.text: The effect runs immediately on the same microtask
The effect is scheduled to re-run asynchronously after the current change detection cycle, executing its side-effect code with the new signal values
The effect only runs once during component initialization
The effect emits a new observable event
To advance real time by 100 milliseconds
To advance the fakeAsync virtual clock by 100ms, resolving the delay(100) in the observable so the async operation completes
To trigger 100 change detection cycles
To wait for 100 HTTP requests to complete
Verifies that the flushed data matches the expected shape
Verifies that all expected HTTP requests were made and that no unexpected requests remain unhandled, failing the test if any are outstanding
Verifies the HTTP status code was 200
Closes the HttpTestingController after tests

questions.text: In Angular routing, what is the difference between { path: '', redirectTo: 'home', pathMatch: 'full' } and { path: '', redirectTo: 'home', pathMatch: 'prefix' }?

options.text: They behave identically for the empty path
pathMatch: 'full' only redirects when the entire URL is empty; pathMatch: 'prefix' redirects when the URL starts with the path (which for '' matches ALL routes, causing infinite redirects)
pathMatch: 'prefix' is required for lazy-loaded modules
pathMatch: 'full' is the default and pathMatch: 'prefix' must be explicitly set for child routes

questions.text: What is the purpose of the mergeMap operator compared to switchMap for uploading multiple files in parallel?

options.text: mergeMap is preferred because it cancels previous uploads when a new file is added
mergeMap is preferred because it subscribes to all inner observables concurrently, allowing multiple file uploads to run in parallel without cancellation
switchMap is preferred for parallel uploads to avoid race conditions
They behave the same for file uploads
async/await is not supported in Angular components
The await resolves outside Angular's zone (or without marking for check in OnPush), so change detection is not triggered; using async pipe or calling markForCheck() would fix it
OnPush components cannot use async/await at all
this.data must be declared as a Signal
declarations compiles the directive; exports is required to avoid type errors
declarations makes the directive available within SharedModule; exports makes it available to any module that imports SharedModule
exports is for testing only; declarations is for production
declarations registers the directive with the router; exports registers it with the DI system

questions.text: In NgRx, what is the on() function inside createReducer used for?

options.text: Subscribing to a store selector inside a reducer
Mapping a specific action to a state transformation function, defining how state changes when that action is dispatched
Registering an effect for the action
Attaching a validator to the action's payload

questions.text: In Angular, what is the inject() function and when would you use it instead of constructor injection?

options.text: inject() is a test utility for injecting mock services
inject() allows retrieving dependencies from Angular's injector at the point of call (e.g., in functional guards, factory functions, or field initializers) without needing constructor parameters
inject() replaces @Injectable for class-based services
inject() is used to inject HTML elements into components
<button type='submit' *ngIf='form.valid'>Submit</button>
<button type='submit' [disabled]='form.invalid'>Submit</button>
<button type='submit' (disabled)='form.invalid'>Submit</button>
<button type='submit' disabled='{{form.invalid}}'>Submit</button>

questions.text: What problem does the async pipe solve compared to manually subscribing in ngOnInit and unsubscribing in ngOnDestroy?

options.text: The async pipe emits faster than manual subscriptions
The async pipe automatically subscribes and unsubscribes, handles change detection for OnPush components, and eliminates boilerplate subscription management code
Manual subscriptions cannot handle errors; async pipe can
The async pipe converts observables to signals automatically

questions.text: What is the key principle behind NgRx's immutable state updates in reducers?

options.text: The reducer must use Object.freeze() on every state object
The reducer must return a new state object (using spread operators or Object.assign) rather than mutating the existing state, ensuring change detection and time-travel debugging work correctly
NgRx automatically makes all state immutable so reducers can mutate freely
State must be stored as a JSON string and parsed on every action
